topics.meaning: Social Science is the study of human society, including how people live, their past, their surroundings, their governance and their economic life.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Social Science helps us understand the world of human beings: how people live together, how societies developed in the past, how the land and environment shape our lives, how we are governed, and how we earn a living. It is made up of several branches that work together. History studies the past, Geography studies the Earth and our environment, Political Science studies government and rights, and Economics studies work, money and resources. By studying Social Science, we learn to understand our society, value our heritage, become responsible citizens and make sense of the world around us.
topics.easyExplanation: Social Science is the study of people and society, past and present. It combines history, geography, political science and economics to help us understand our world.

Studying mountains, rivers and maps is part of Geography.

topics.meaning: A map is a drawing of the Earth or a part of it on a flat surface, and its main components are distance (scale), direction and symbols.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A map is a representation of the Earth, or a part of it, drawn to scale on a flat surface. Maps help us find places and understand an area. Every map has three main components. The first is distance, shown using a scale, which tells us how a small distance on the map relates to the real distance on the ground. The second is direction, usually shown with the four main directions (north, south, east, west) and a north arrow. The third is symbols, which are signs and colours used to show features like roads, rivers, railways and buildings, explained in a key or legend. Maps are more useful than a globe for studying small areas in detail.
topics.easyExplanation: A map is a flat drawing of the Earth. It uses a scale for distance, directions like north and south, and symbols (with a key) to show roads, rivers and places.

topics.meaning: A globe is a model of the Earth, and a grid of lines called latitudes and longitudes helps us locate any place on it.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A globe is a small, round model of the Earth that shows the correct shapes and positions of land and water. To locate places exactly, a network of imaginary lines is drawn on the globe. The lines running from east to west are called latitudes (or parallels), and the lines running from north to south, joining the two poles, are called longitudes (or meridians). Together these lines form a grid. Using the latitude and longitude of a place, we can find its exact location on the Earth, just like an address.
topics.easyExplanation: A globe is a model of the Earth. Imaginary lines called latitudes (east-west) and longitudes (north-south) form a grid that helps us find any place's exact location.

topics.meaning: Latitudes are measured in degrees from the Equator, and some important latitudes include the Equator, the Tropics and the Poles.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Latitudes are measured in degrees north or south of the Equator. The Equator is the most important latitude, at 0 degrees, and it divides the Earth into the Northern Hemisphere and the Southern Hemisphere. Latitudes increase from 0 degrees at the Equator to 90 degrees at the North Pole and 90 degrees at the South Pole. Some special latitudes are the Tropic of Cancer (north of the Equator), the Tropic of Capricorn (south of the Equator), and the Arctic and Antarctic Circles near the poles. These important latitudes help us understand the Earth's heat zones and climate.

topics.meaning: Longitudes are measured in degrees east or west of the Prime Meridian, which is the 0-degree longitude.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Longitudes are the lines running from the North Pole to the South Pole. They are measured in degrees east or west of a starting line called the Prime Meridian, which is at 0 degrees and passes through Greenwich in London. Unlike latitudes, all longitudes are of equal length. Longitudes go up to 180 degrees east and 180 degrees west, which meet on the opposite side of the Earth. The Prime Meridian divides the Earth into the Eastern Hemisphere and the Western Hemisphere. Longitudes are closely connected to the measurement of time around the world.
topics.easyExplanation: Longitudes run from pole to pole and are measured east or west of the Prime Meridian (0 degrees) at Greenwich. They are all equal in length and relate to time.

topics.meaning: Because the Earth rotates, different places have different local times, so the world is divided into time zones based on longitude.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The Earth rotates from west to east, so the Sun appears to rise earlier in places to the east. This means different longitudes have different local times. To manage this, the world is divided into time zones, each based on longitude and measured from the time at the Prime Meridian, called Greenwich Mean Time (GMT). A large country may choose one standard time for the whole country; India follows Indian Standard Time (IST), based on a longitude passing near Mirzapur. Understanding time zones helps us know the time in different parts of the world.
topics.easyExplanation: The Earth spins, so different places have different times. The world is split into time zones based on longitude. India uses one standard time, IST.

topics.meaning: The Earth's surface is made up of large landmasses called continents and huge water bodies called oceans, with much more water than land.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When we look at the Earth from space, we see that most of its surface is covered with water, which is why it is often called the 'Blue Planet'. The Earth's surface is divided into two main kinds of features: land and water. The very large stretches of land are called continents, and the very large bodies of salt water are called oceans. About two-thirds of the Earth's surface is water and only about one-third is land. Continents and oceans are located using latitudes and longitudes.

topics.meaning: There are seven continents on the Earth: Asia, Africa, North America, South America, Antarctica, Europe and Australia.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The large landmasses of the Earth are divided into seven continents. In order of size, they are Asia (the largest), Africa, North America, South America, Antarctica, Europe and Australia (the smallest). Asia is the largest continent and is home to India. Antarctica, around the South Pole, is covered with thick ice and has no permanent human population. Each continent has its own landforms, climate, plants, animals and people. The continents are separated by oceans but some are joined by land.

topics.meaning: There are five oceans on the Earth: the Pacific, Atlantic, Indian, Southern and Arctic Oceans.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The huge bodies of salt water on the Earth are divided into five oceans. The Pacific Ocean is the largest and deepest, followed by the Atlantic Ocean, the Indian Ocean (which touches India's southern coast), the Southern Ocean (around Antarctica) and the Arctic Ocean (around the North Pole, the smallest). All the oceans are connected to one another, forming one continuous body of water. Oceans are very important: they affect climate and weather, are home to countless living things, and are used for trade, fishing and travel.

topics.meaning: Oceans and continents support life by providing land to live on, water, food, resources, climate balance and routes for trade.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Continents and oceans together make life on Earth possible. The continents provide land where people, plants and animals live, and give us soil for farming, forests, minerals and other resources. The oceans provide a home to a vast variety of sea life, supply fish and other food, influence the climate and rainfall, and serve as important routes for ships carrying goods and people. Both continents and oceans are precious, and we must protect them from pollution and damage so that they can continue to support life.
topics.easyExplanation: Continents give us land, soil and resources to live, while oceans give food, control climate and carry ships. Both are precious and must be protected.

topics.meaning: The main landforms of the Earth are mountains, plateaus and plains, which differ in height and shape.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The surface of the Earth is not flat everywhere; it has different shapes called landforms. The three major landforms are mountains, plateaus and plains. Mountains are very high landforms with steep slopes and pointed peaks. Plateaus are raised areas of fairly level land that stand higher than the land around them, like a tabletop. Plains are large stretches of flat, low land. These landforms are created over a very long time by natural forces, and each one offers a different kind of place for people, plants and animals to live.
topics.easyExplanation: The Earth has three main landforms: mountains (high and steep), plateaus (raised flat lands) and plains (low flat lands). Each is a different place to live.

topics.meaning: Mountains are high landforms that influence climate, rivers and the way people live in mountain regions.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Mountains are the highest landforms, often with snow-covered peaks. They have a great effect on life. Many rivers begin in the mountains from melting snow and glaciers, supplying water to the lands below. Mountains also affect the climate and can cause rainfall. Living in the mountains is challenging because of steep slopes, cold weather and thin air, so the population is usually small. People in mountain regions practise terrace farming, rear animals, and the beautiful scenery supports tourism. Mountains are also rich in forests and wildlife.
topics.easyExplanation: Mountains are the highest landforms. They give us rivers from melting snow, affect climate, and people there do terrace farming, rear animals and welcome tourists.

topics.meaning: Plateaus are raised, fairly level lands that are often rich in minerals and support farming and mining.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A plateau is a raised area of land with a fairly flat top, standing higher than the land around it, which is why it is sometimes called a tableland. Plateaus are often rich in minerals such as iron, coal and gold, so mining is an important activity in many plateau regions. The soil on some plateaus, like the black soil of the Deccan Plateau, is good for growing crops such as cotton. Waterfalls are also common where rivers drop from the edge of a plateau. Plateaus thus support mining, farming and sometimes the generation of electricity.
topics.easyExplanation: Plateaus are raised flat lands, like tabletops. They are rich in minerals for mining, have good soil for crops, and often have waterfalls.

topics.meaning: Plains are flat, low and fertile lands where most people live and where farming is the main activity.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Plains are large stretches of flat, low-lying land. Many plains are formed by rivers depositing fertile soil, which makes them very good for farming. Because the land is flat and the soil is fertile, plains are the most thickly populated landforms, with many villages, towns and cities. The flat land also makes it easy to build roads, railways and houses, and to carry out transport and trade. Most of the world's food is grown on plains, which is why they are sometimes called the food bowls of the world.
topics.easyExplanation: Plains are flat, low, fertile lands. Most people live here because farming is easy and it is simple to build roads and cities. They grow most of our food.

topics.meaning: History measures time over long periods using a timeline and the terms BCE (Before Common Era) and CE (Common Era).
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: History deals with events that happened over thousands of years, so we need a way to measure and order time. A timeline is a line on which events are marked in the order they happened, helping us see what came before and after. Years are counted from a fixed point. Years before this point are written as BCE (Before Common Era) and years after it as CE (Common Era). In the BCE period, larger numbers mean longer ago, so 2000 BCE is earlier than 500 BCE. We also group long periods into units like decades (10 years), centuries (100 years) and millennia (1000 years). This helps us place historical events correctly in time.

topics.meaning: We learn about the past from sources, which are mainly archaeological sources (objects and remains) and literary sources (written records).
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Historians find out about the past using clues called sources. There are two main kinds. Archaeological sources are physical remains from the past, such as tools, pottery, coins, buildings, monuments and bones, which are often dug out of the ground. Literary sources are written records, such as old books, manuscripts, inscriptions on stone or metal, and letters. By studying and comparing these sources carefully, historians piece together the story of the past. Each source gives us part of the picture, and using many sources together makes our understanding of history more reliable.

topics.meaning: Our country has several names, including Bharat, India and Hindustan, each with its own history.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Our country is known by more than one name. The name Bharat comes from ancient texts and is linked to legendary figures and tribes mentioned in old Indian literature; it is the name used in many Indian languages and in the Constitution. The name India comes from the river Sindhu (the Indus); the Persians called it Hindu, the Greeks called the land beyond it Indos or India, and so the name India developed. The name Hindustan also grew from the word Hindu used for the land around the Sindhu river. So all these names are connected to the land, its rivers and its long history.

topics.meaning: The different names of India show how outsiders and insiders saw the land, and reflect its unity and long, continuous history.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The many names of our country tell us a great deal about its history. They show that India has been known to many peoples, such as the Persians and the Greeks, for thousands of years, often through its great river the Sindhu. They also show a sense of unity, as the whole land came to be known by shared names. The continued use of the name Bharat connects modern India to its ancient past and culture. Studying these names helps us understand that India is one of the world's oldest civilisations with a long and continuous identity.

topics.topic: From Hunting to the First Crops
topics.meaning: Early humans first lived by hunting and gathering, then learned farming and herding, which led to settled village life.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The earliest humans were hunter-gatherers, who got their food by hunting animals and gathering fruits, roots and seeds, and moved from place to place in search of food. Over a very long time, people learned to grow crops and to tame and rear animals. This was a huge change: once people could grow their own food, they no longer needed to wander, and they began to settle down in one place. Growing the first crops led to the rise of permanent villages, new tools, pottery and a more organised way of life. This shift from hunting to farming was the first step towards civilisation.
topics.easyExplanation: Early humans hunted and gathered food and kept moving. When they learned to grow crops and rear animals, they settled in villages, the first step to civilisation.

topics.meaning: The Indus-Sarasvati or Harappan Civilisation was one of the world's earliest civilisations, which grew along the Sindhu and Sarasvati rivers.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: About 5,000 years ago, one of the world's earliest civilisations developed in the north-western part of the Indian subcontinent. It is called the Harappan Civilisation, after the first city found at Harappa, and also the Indus-Sarasvati Civilisation because its cities grew along the Sindhu (Indus) and the now-dried-up Sarasvati rivers. It had many well-planned cities, such as Harappa, Mohenjo-daro and Rakhigarhi (in present-day Haryana). The people grew crops, kept animals, made beautiful pottery and ornaments, and traded goods. This civilisation shows that India had advanced cities thousands of years ago.

topics.meaning: Harappan cities were famous for their excellent town planning, with well-laid streets, brick houses and good drainage.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The Harappans were remarkable town planners. Their cities were carefully laid out, with streets that crossed each other at right angles, dividing the city into neat blocks. Houses were built of baked bricks of standard sizes, and many had their own wells and bathrooms. A striking feature was the well-planned drainage system, with covered drains running along the streets to carry away waste water. Cities often had a raised area (citadel) and a lower town, and large structures like the Great Bath. The Harappans also made fine pottery, jewellery, seals and toys, showing skilled craftsmanship.

topics.meaning: After flourishing for centuries, the Harappan Civilisation slowly declined, likely due to climate and river changes.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The Harappan Civilisation flourished for many centuries and then slowly declined. Historians are not fully certain why, but several reasons are suggested. Major changes in climate may have made the region drier. The drying up of the Sarasvati river and changes in the course of rivers would have harmed farming and water supply. There may also have been floods or a fall in trade. As conditions worsened, people gradually left the great cities and moved to smaller settlements, often towards the east. The decline reminds us how closely human life depends on rivers and climate.

topics.meaning: The Vedas are the oldest sacred texts of India, and Vedic culture shaped much of India's early thought and way of life.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The Vedas are among the oldest texts in the world and are the foundation of much of Indian culture. There are four Vedas, the oldest being the Rigveda, which is a collection of hymns. The Vedas were composed in Sanskrit and were passed down by being carefully memorised and recited aloud for many generations before being written down. They tell us about the beliefs, prayers, daily life, and ideas of the people of the Vedic period. Vedic culture valued nature, truth and learning, and many of its ideas, prayers and values continue to influence Indian life and thought today.

topics.topic: Other Roots: New Ideas and Teachers
topics.meaning: India's cultural roots also include new schools of thought and great teachers, such as those of Jainism and Buddhism.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Besides the Vedic tradition, India's cultural roots include many other ideas and great teachers who explored deep questions about life, truth and right conduct. Among them were the founders and teachers of Jainism and Buddhism. Mahavira spread the teachings of Jainism, which stresses non-violence (ahimsa) and truth. Gautama Buddha founded Buddhism, teaching the middle path, kindness and the end of suffering. These teachers used simple languages so ordinary people could understand them. Together with the Vedic tradition, these ideas made India a land of many philosophies and a great centre of thought and learning.

topics.meaning: India's culture is connected to its land, with many rivers, mountains and places treated as sacred and linking the country together.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: An important part of India's cultural roots is its sacred geography, the idea that the land itself is holy. Across India, rivers such as the Ganga and Yamuna, mountains like the Himalayas, and many towns and places are regarded as sacred. People travel on pilgrimages to these places from all over the country. This network of sacred sites, spread from the north to the south and the east to the west, has helped tie the different parts of India together over thousands of years. Sacred geography shows how India's culture, faith and land are deeply connected.

chapters.chapter: Unity in Diversity, or 'Many in the One'

topics.meaning: India has enormous diversity in its languages, religions, food, clothing, festivals and ways of life.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: India is one of the most diverse countries in the world. Its people speak hundreds of different languages and dialects, follow many religions, and celebrate a huge number of festivals. Food, clothing, music, dance and customs change from one region to another, so the way of life in one part of India can look very different from another. This variety is found not only between regions but even within a single town or village. India's diversity is one of its greatest features and makes it a colourful and fascinating country.

topics.meaning: Despite its diversity, India is bound together by shared values, festivals, sacred geography, history and a sense of being one nation.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Even though India is so diverse, many threads bind its people together into one nation. These include a shared history and culture, common values such as respect for elders and hospitality, and the sacred geography that links holy places across the land. Many festivals, stories from the great epics like the Ramayana and Mahabharata, and similar customs are found in different forms all over India. The Constitution and a shared sense of being Indian also unite people. These common threads running through India's diversity create its famous 'unity in diversity'.
topics.easyExplanation: Even with so much variety, Indians share values, festivals, epics, sacred places and history. These shared threads bind the country into one nation.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Shared history and culture
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A common history and culture connect people across India.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Shared values
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Values like respect for elders and hospitality are found everywhere.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Shared stories and sacred places
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Epics like the Ramayana and Mahabharata and sacred geography link the whole land.

stepByStepExplanation.title: One nation
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The Constitution and a shared sense of being Indian unite the people.

importantKeywords.definition: A long story about heroes, like the Ramayana.

topics.meaning: 'Unity in diversity' means that India's many differences exist together within one united nation.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The phrase 'unity in diversity' describes India perfectly. It means that even though Indians differ greatly in language, religion, food and customs, they live together as one united nation, sharing common bonds and a single identity. The idea is also expressed as 'many in the one', many different parts forming one whole. This unity in diversity is a great strength: it makes India rich and colourful while keeping it together. To keep this strength, people must respect one another's differences and live together with harmony, tolerance and friendship.
topics.easyExplanation: 'Unity in diversity' means India's many differences live together as one nation. It is a strength, kept alive by respect, tolerance and harmony.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Meaning of the phrase
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: 'Unity in diversity' means many differences existing together in one united nation.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Many in the one

stepByStepExplanation.title: A great strength
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: This unity in diversity makes India rich, colourful and strong.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Keeping it alive
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: It is preserved by respecting differences and living with harmony and tolerance.

examples: People of many languages and religions live together as one Indian nation.
India's motto 'unity in diversity' is seen in its many festivals celebrated by all.

importantKeywords.definition: Many differences existing together in one united nation.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: India is famous for unity in diversity.

importantKeywords.definition: Accepting and respecting differences.

importantKeywords.definition: Living together peacefully.

topics.meaning: A family is a group of people related by blood or marriage, and families can be of different types, such as nuclear and joint families.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A family is one of the first and most important groups a person belongs to. It is a group of people connected by blood, marriage or adoption, who usually live together and care for one another. Families can be of different kinds. A nuclear family is small, with parents and their children. A joint family is larger, with parents, children, grandparents and other relatives like uncles and aunts living together. Families give us love, support, food, shelter and our first lessons in values and behaviour. Over time, family structures change, but the family remains a basic unit of society.
topics.easyExplanation: A family is people related by blood or marriage who live together and care for each other. A nuclear family is small (parents and children); a joint family is bigger.

stepByStepExplanation.title: What a family is
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A family is a group related by blood, marriage or adoption who care for one another.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Nuclear family
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A small family of parents and their children.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Joint family
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A larger family of parents, children, grandparents and other relatives together.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Why family matters
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Families give love, support, shelter and our first lessons in values.

examples: A mother, father and their children form a nuclear family.
Grandparents, parents, children, uncles and aunts living together form a joint family.

importantKeywords.definition: A group of people related by blood, marriage or adoption.

importantKeywords.definition: A small family of parents and children.

importantKeywords.definition: A large family with several generations together.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A joint family includes grandparents and relatives.

topics.meaning: A community is a larger group of people who live in the same area or share something in common, and it helps and supports its members.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Beyond the family is a larger group called the community. A community is a group of people who live in the same area, such as a village or a neighbourhood, or who share something in common, such as language, work, interests or beliefs. Members of a community help and support one another, share resources like water and roads, and work together for common needs. People can belong to more than one community at the same time. Living in a community teaches us cooperation, sharing and responsibility, and helps everyone live a better life together.
topics.easyExplanation: A community is a bigger group sharing the same place or something in common, like language or work. People in a community help each other and share resources.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Beyond the family

stepByStepExplanation.title: Who forms a community
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: People who live in the same area or share language, work or beliefs form a community.

stepByStepExplanation.title: They help each other
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Community members support one another and share resources like water and roads.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Belonging to many
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A person can belong to several communities at once, learning cooperation and responsibility.

examples: The people of a village form a community that shares roads and water.
People who follow the same profession can form a community.

topics.meaning: A government is the group of people and institutions that make decisions, run a country and maintain order for the welfare of the people.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A government is needed wherever people live together, to make rules, take decisions and look after everyone's welfare. The government makes laws, provides services like schools, hospitals, roads, water and electricity, keeps order and protects the country. Without a government, there would be confusion and no one to settle disputes or provide common services. The government works for the people, and in return people follow its laws. Studying government helps us understand how decisions that affect our daily lives are made.
topics.easyExplanation: A government is the group that makes rules, runs the country and provides services like schools, roads and water for everyone's welfare.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Why we need it
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Wherever people live together, a government is needed to make rules and decisions.

stepByStepExplanation.title: What it does
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The government makes laws, provides services and keeps order.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Provides services
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: It gives services like schools, hospitals, roads, water and electricity.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Works for the people
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The government works for the welfare of the people, who in turn follow its laws.

examples: The government builds roads and runs government schools and hospitals.
The government makes laws that everyone must follow.

importantKeywords.definition: The group that makes decisions and runs a country.

importantKeywords.definition: The health, happiness and good of the people.

topics.meaning: Government in India works at three levels: the central (national), the state, and the local level.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Because India is a very large country, the government works at three levels so that decisions can be made closer to the people. The central government, also called the national or union government, looks after the whole country and matters like defence and foreign affairs. The state government looks after a single state and matters like the state's schools, police and roads. The local government works at the smallest level, in villages and towns, handling local needs like water supply, sanitation and street lights. These three levels work together to govern the country effectively.
topics.easyExplanation: India's government works at three levels: central (whole country), state (one state), and local (villages and towns). Each handles different matters.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Three levels
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Government in India works at three levels because the country is so large.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Central government
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The national government looks after the whole country and matters like defence.

stepByStepExplanation.title: State government
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The state government looks after one state's schools, police and roads.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Local government
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The local government handles village and town needs like water and street lights.

examples: The central government handles defence for the whole country.
The local government looks after street lights and water supply in your area.

importantKeywords.definition: The national government for the whole country.

importantKeywords.definition: The government of a single state.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: The state government runs state schools.

importantKeywords.definition: The government for a village or town.

revisionNotes: Three levels of government in India.

topics.meaning: Government has three organs: the legislature that makes laws, the executive that carries them out, and the judiciary that settles disputes.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A government does its work through three parts, called organs. The legislature is the body that makes laws; it is made up of people's elected representatives. The executive is the part that puts the laws into action and runs the day-to-day work of government. The judiciary is the system of courts that settles disputes, ensures justice and makes sure laws are followed fairly. These three organs have separate roles but work together. Keeping their roles separate helps prevent the misuse of power and protects the rights of the people.
topics.easyExplanation: Government has three organs: the legislature makes laws, the executive carries them out, and the judiciary (courts) settles disputes and gives justice.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Three organs

stepByStepExplanation.title: Executive
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The executive carries out the laws and runs the daily work of government.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Judiciary
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The judiciary, the courts, settles disputes and ensures justice.

examples: The legislature debates and passes new laws.

topics.meaning: Democracy is government by the people through elected representatives, and grassroots democracy gives people a say at the local level.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Democracy is a form of government in which the people rule, usually by electing their representatives to make decisions on their behalf. In a democracy, every adult has the right to vote, and the government is answerable to the people. Grassroots democracy means democracy at the lowest, local level, in villages and towns, where ordinary people take part directly in making decisions about their own area. This brings government close to the people and lets them solve their local problems themselves. Grassroots democracy is an important way for citizens to participate in running their community.
topics.easyExplanation: Democracy means rule by the people through elected representatives. Grassroots democracy brings this to the local level, letting people decide about their own area.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Grassroots democracy

stepByStepExplanation.title: People participate
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: It lets ordinary people take part directly in decisions about their own area.

examples: People elect their representatives by voting in a democracy.
Villagers deciding together how to spend funds on local needs is grassroots democracy.

importantKeywords.definition: Democracy at the local level.

chapters.chapter: Grassroots Democracy - Part 2: Local Government in Rural Areas

topics.topic: The Gram Sabha and Gram Panchayat
topics.meaning: In villages, local government is run by the Gram Panchayat, which is guided by the Gram Sabha of all village voters.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: In rural (village) areas, local self-government is carried out mainly through the Gram Sabha and the Gram Panchayat. The Gram Sabha is a meeting of all the adult voters of a village; it is the foundation of village democracy, where people discuss and approve plans and keep an eye on the work of their leaders. The Gram Panchayat is the elected council that runs the village from day to day. It is headed by the Sarpanch (or Pradhan) and includes elected members called Panches. The Gram Sabha elects the Panchayat and holds it answerable, making sure the village is run for everyone's benefit.
topics.easyExplanation: In villages, the Gram Sabha (all adult voters) is the base of local democracy, and it elects the Gram Panchayat, led by the Sarpanch, to run the village.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Village self-government

stepByStepExplanation.title: The Gram Panchayat

topics.topic: The Three Tiers of Panchayati Raj
topics.meaning: Rural local government has three tiers: the Gram Panchayat (village), the Panchayat Samiti (block) and the Zila Parishad (district).
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Rural local self-government in India is organised in a three-tier (three-level) system called Panchayati Raj. At the lowest level is the Gram Panchayat, which works for one village or a group of small villages. At the middle level is the Panchayat Samiti (also called the block or intermediate level), which works for a group of Gram Panchayats. At the highest level is the Zila Parishad, which works for the whole district and coordinates the work of the Panchayat Samitis. This three-tier system helps plan and carry out development work across villages, blocks and districts.
topics.easyExplanation: Panchayati Raj has three tiers: Gram Panchayat (village), Panchayat Samiti (block of villages), and Zila Parishad (whole district).

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Rural local government, called Panchayati Raj, has three levels.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Gram Panchayat

stepByStepExplanation.title: Panchayat Samiti
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The middle tier, at the block level, works for a group of Gram Panchayats.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Zila Parishad
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The highest tier works for the whole district and coordinates the Panchayat Samitis.

topics.topic: Roles and Responsibilities of the Panchayat
topics.meaning: The Gram Panchayat looks after local services and development, and gets its money from taxes, fees and government grants.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The Gram Panchayat has many roles and responsibilities for the welfare of the village. It looks after local needs such as supplying clean water, building and repairing roads and drains, providing street lighting, keeping the village clean, and helping run schools and health services. It also keeps records like births and deaths. To do this work, the Panchayat needs money, which it gets from local taxes and fees, and from grants given by the state and central governments. By carrying out these responsibilities, the Panchayat helps improve daily life in the village.
topics.easyExplanation: The Gram Panchayat provides village services like water, roads, drains, street lights and cleanliness. It pays for this through local taxes, fees and government grants.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Looks after the village

chapters.chapter: Grassroots Democracy - Part 3: Local Government in Urban Areas

topics.meaning: In towns and cities, local government is run by urban local bodies, which look after the needs of urban areas.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Just as villages have Panchayats, towns and cities have their own local governments called urban local bodies. They are needed because urban areas have large populations and special needs, such as managing traffic, water supply, garbage, street lighting and public health for many people living close together. The type of urban local body depends on the size of the town or city. These bodies are run by representatives elected by the people of the urban area, making them another form of grassroots democracy that brings government close to city dwellers.
topics.easyExplanation: Towns and cities have local governments called urban local bodies. They manage city needs like water, garbage, traffic and lighting, and are elected by the people.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Towns and cities have local governments called urban local bodies.

topics.meaning: Urban areas are governed by Municipal Corporations in large cities, Municipal Councils in smaller towns, and Nagar Panchayats in very small towns.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: There are different kinds of urban local bodies for different sizes of urban areas. A Municipal Corporation governs a very large city, with many departments and a large team to manage the city's needs. A Municipal Council (or Municipality) governs a smaller town. A Nagar Panchayat governs a very small or newly growing town that is becoming urban. The elected head of a Municipal Corporation is usually called the Mayor, while the city's daily administration is run by appointed officials. These bodies divide the city into wards, each represented by an elected councillor.
topics.easyExplanation: Big cities have Municipal Corporations, smaller towns have Municipal Councils, and very small towns have Nagar Panchayats. A Mayor often heads a Corporation.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Municipal Council and Nagar Panchayat

topics.meaning: Urban local bodies provide city services and raise money through taxes, fees and government grants.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Urban local bodies do a great deal of work to keep a town or city running. They provide and maintain services such as water supply, drainage and sewage, garbage collection and disposal, street lighting, roads, parks, public health and the control of diseases. To pay for all this, they raise money from taxes such as property tax, from fees and charges for services, and from grants given by the state and central governments. The elected councillors and the appointed officials work together so that the city is clean, safe and well run for all its residents.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Provide city services

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Elected councillors and appointed officials work together to run the city well.

Funded by property tax, fees and grants.

topics.meaning: Work is any activity done with a purpose, and all honest work has value and dignity.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Work is any activity that people do with a purpose, usually to produce goods or provide services, or to look after themselves and others. Every kind of honest work is valuable, whether it is done by a farmer, a teacher, a doctor, a cleaner or a homemaker. Work meets our needs, keeps society running, and gives people a sense of pride and self-respect. The idea of the dignity of work means that no honest work is high or low; all work deserves respect. Understanding the value of work helps us respect every worker and the contribution they make.
topics.easyExplanation: Work is any purposeful activity. All honest work, big or small, has value and deserves respect. This is called the dignity of work.

stepByStepExplanation.title: What work is
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Work is any purposeful activity done to make goods, give services or care for others.

stepByStepExplanation.title: All work is valuable
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Every kind of honest work, from farming to teaching to cleaning, has value.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Work helps society
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Work meets our needs and keeps society running, giving people pride and self-respect.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Dignity of work
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The dignity of work means no honest work is high or low; all work deserves respect.

examples: A farmer growing food and a teacher teaching children are both doing valuable work.
A homemaker's work of caring for the family is also valuable work.

importantKeywords.simpleExample: Cooking food is a kind of work.

importantKeywords.definition: The idea that all honest work deserves respect.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Dignity of work means respecting every worker.

importantKeywords.definition: Pride and respect for oneself.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Honest work gives a person self-respect.

topics.quickSummary: Work is any purposeful activity, and all honest work, from farming to teaching to cleaning, has value and dignity; the dignity of work means every worker deserves respect.
revisionNotes: Work = any purposeful activity.
All honest work has value.
Work meets needs and gives self-respect.

topics.meaning: Economic activities are done to earn money or a living, while non-economic activities are done without the aim of earning money.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The work people do can be divided into two kinds. Economic activities are those done to earn money or make a living, such as a shopkeeper selling goods, a farmer selling crops or a doctor treating patients for a fee. Non-economic activities are done not to earn money but out of love, duty or social purpose, such as a mother caring for her child, helping a neighbour, or doing voluntary social service. Both types of work are important. Even though non-economic activities do not earn money, they are very valuable for the family and society.
topics.easyExplanation: Economic activities earn money (like selling goods). Non-economic activities are done without earning money (like caring for family). Both are important.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Two kinds of work
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Work can be divided into economic and non-economic activities.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Economic activities
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: These are done to earn money or a living, like selling goods or treating patients for a fee.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Non-economic activities
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: These are done without earning money, out of love, duty or social purpose.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Both are important
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Even though non-economic activities earn no money, they are very valuable for family and society.

examples: A shopkeeper selling goods is doing an economic activity.
A mother caring for her child is a non-economic activity.

importantKeywords.definition: Work done to earn money or a living.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Selling vegetables is an economic activity.

importantKeywords.definition: Work done without the aim of earning money.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Helping a neighbour is a non-economic activity.

importantKeywords.definition: Money earned from work.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A shopkeeper earns income from sales.

topics.quickSummary: Work is of two kinds: economic activities, done to earn money (like selling goods), and non-economic activities, done without earning money (like caring for family); both are important.
revisionNotes: Economic activity = done to earn money.
Non-economic activity = done without earning money.
Examples: selling goods vs caring for family.
Both kinds of work are valuable.

topics.meaning: Non-economic activities, though unpaid, are essential because they support families, communities and society.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Non-economic activities do not earn money, but they are essential to our lives. Much of the work done at home, such as cooking, cleaning, caring for children and looking after elders, is non-economic, yet without it families could not function. Voluntary work in the community, like helping the needy, keeping public places clean or teaching for free, also makes society better. Such work is often not counted in money terms and may go unnoticed, but it has great social value. Recognising the importance of non-economic activities helps us value all those who do this unpaid but vital work.
topics.easyExplanation: Non-economic activities are unpaid, like housework and helping others, but they are essential. Families and society could not run without them.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Unpaid but essential
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Non-economic activities earn no money but are essential to our lives.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Work at home
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Cooking, cleaning and caring for family are non-economic but keep families running.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Helping the community
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Voluntary work like helping the needy or cleaning public places improves society.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Great social value
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Such work is often unnoticed and uncounted in money, but has great social value.

examples: Caring for elders and children at home is vital non-economic work.
Volunteers cleaning a park do valuable non-economic work.

importantKeywords.definition: Work done without earning money.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Housework is a non-economic activity.

importantKeywords.definition: Work done willingly without pay to help others.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Cleaning a park for free is voluntary work.

importantKeywords.definition: The benefit something brings to society.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Caring for elders has great social value.

topics.quickSummary: Non-economic activities, though unpaid, are essential because housework and voluntary community work keep families and society running, giving them great social value.
revisionNotes: Non-economic activities are unpaid but essential.

topics.meaning: Economic activities are classified into three types: primary, secondary and tertiary activities.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: All the economic activities that produce goods and services can be grouped into three main types. Primary activities use natural resources directly from the Earth, such as farming, fishing, mining and forestry. Secondary activities take the products of primary activities and turn them into new goods, such as making cloth from cotton or furniture from wood; this is also called manufacturing. Tertiary activities provide services rather than goods, such as transport, banking, teaching and trade. These three types depend on one another and together make up the economy. Understanding this classification helps us see how goods and services are produced.
topics.easyExplanation: Economic activities are of three types: primary (taking from nature, like farming), secondary (making goods, like factories), and tertiary (services, like transport).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Three types
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Economic activities are grouped into primary, secondary and tertiary types.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Primary
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Primary activities use natural resources directly, like farming, fishing and mining.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Secondary
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Secondary activities turn raw materials into new goods, like making cloth from cotton.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Tertiary
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Tertiary activities provide services, like transport, banking and teaching.

examples: Farming is a primary activity, making cloth is secondary, and running a bank is tertiary.
Turning cotton into cloth is a secondary (manufacturing) activity.

importantKeywords.definition: An activity that uses natural resources directly.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Fishing is a primary activity.

importantKeywords.definition: An activity that makes goods from raw materials.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Making furniture is a secondary activity.

importantKeywords.definition: An activity that provides services.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Teaching is a tertiary activity.

topics.quickSummary: Economic activities are classified into primary (using natural resources, like farming), secondary (making goods, like manufacturing) and tertiary (providing services, like transport and banking).
revisionNotes: Three types: primary, secondary, tertiary.
Primary = uses natural resources (farming, mining).

topics.meaning: Primary activities produce goods directly from nature, such as agriculture, fishing, mining, forestry and animal rearing.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Primary activities are those in which people obtain goods directly from nature. The most important primary activity is agriculture (farming), which gives us food grains, vegetables, fruits and raw materials like cotton. Other primary activities include fishing, rearing animals (like cows, goats and poultry), mining minerals from the Earth, and collecting forest products. A large number of people in India depend on primary activities, especially farming, for their living. Primary activities form the base of the economy because they provide food and the raw materials needed by other activities.
topics.easyExplanation: Primary activities take goods straight from nature, like farming, fishing, mining and rearing animals. Many Indians, especially farmers, depend on them.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Goods from nature
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Primary activities obtain goods directly from nature.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Agriculture
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Farming is the most important primary activity, giving food and raw materials like cotton.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Other primary activities
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: These include fishing, rearing animals, mining minerals and collecting forest products.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Base of the economy
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Many Indians depend on primary activities, which provide food and raw materials for other work.

examples: A farmer growing wheat is doing a primary activity.
Mining coal and rearing cattle are also primary activities.

importantKeywords.definition: An activity that produces goods directly from nature.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Farming is a primary activity.

importantKeywords.definition: The growing of crops and rearing of animals.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Agriculture is India's main primary activity.

importantKeywords.definition: Taking minerals out of the Earth.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Mining coal is a primary activity.

topics.quickSummary: Primary activities produce goods directly from nature, including agriculture, fishing, mining, forestry and animal rearing; many Indians depend on them, especially farming.
revisionNotes: Primary activities take goods directly from nature.
Agriculture is the main one.
Also fishing, mining, forestry, animal rearing.
Many Indians depend on farming for a living.

topics.meaning: Secondary activities turn raw materials from primary activities into finished goods, which is called manufacturing.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Secondary activities take the raw materials produced by primary activities and change them into more useful, finished goods. This process is called manufacturing. For example, cotton from farms is turned into cloth and clothing in textile factories, sugarcane is made into sugar, iron ore is made into steel, and wood is made into furniture. Secondary activities add value to raw materials, so the finished goods are worth more than the raw materials. These activities are carried out in workshops, small industries and large factories, and they provide jobs to many people.
topics.easyExplanation: Secondary activities make finished goods from raw materials, like turning cotton into cloth. This is manufacturing, done in factories and workshops.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Use raw materials
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Secondary activities take raw materials from primary activities.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Make finished goods
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: They turn these raw materials into useful finished goods, called manufacturing.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Add value
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Finished goods are worth more than the raw materials, so value is added.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Provide jobs
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: These activities happen in workshops and factories and give jobs to many people.

examples: Turning cotton into cloth in a textile factory is a secondary activity.
Making furniture from wood and steel from iron are secondary activities.

importantKeywords.definition: An activity that makes finished goods from raw materials.

importantKeywords.definition: The making of goods from raw materials.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Manufacturing turns cotton into cloth.

importantKeywords.simpleExample: Cotton is a raw material for cloth.

topics.quickSummary: Secondary activities turn raw materials from primary activities into finished goods through manufacturing, like cotton into cloth, adding value and providing jobs in factories and workshops.

This is called manufacturing.

topics.meaning: Tertiary activities provide services that support primary and secondary activities, such as transport, trade, banking and education.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Tertiary activities do not produce goods but provide services that help people and support the other activities. They include transport (moving goods and people), trade (buying and selling, including retail), banking and finance, communication, education, health care and tourism. For example, lorries and trains carry goods from factories to markets, shops sell goods to customers, and banks keep money safe and give loans. Tertiary activities are growing fast and are very important in a modern economy, providing jobs to a large and increasing number of people.
topics.easyExplanation: Tertiary activities provide services, not goods, like transport, shops, banks, schools and hospitals. They support other activities and are growing fast.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Provide services
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Tertiary activities provide services rather than producing goods.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Many kinds
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: They include transport, trade, banking, communication, education and health care.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Support other activities
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: For example, transport carries goods to markets and shops sell them to customers.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Growing importance
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Tertiary activities are growing fast and provide jobs to many people in a modern economy.

examples: A bank keeping money safe and giving loans is a tertiary activity.
Transport of goods by trucks and selling them in shops are tertiary activities.

importantKeywords.definition: An activity that provides services.

importantKeywords.definition: The buying and selling of goods.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A shop carries out trade.

importantKeywords.definition: The movement of goods and people.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Trains provide transport.
